narrative: I'm sorry to share that your law school application was rejected because our predictive model estimates a 49% chance you'll fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which is just over our threshold for approval. You have a solid academic profile, including being in the 7th decile for grades in both your first and third years of school—better than the typical student's 6th decile or so—along with a strong LSAT score of 40, well above the average of about 37, and an undergraduate GPA of 3.3, slightly higher than the usual 3.2. Coming from an upper-middle-class family puts you ahead of the dataset's average income bracket, and as a white female planning to attend part-time, you're in line with common demographics, though part-time is less typical at just 7% of students. Despite these strengths, the model's decision was swayed most by your third-year grade decile, which nudged the prediction toward expecting a bar failure. Right behind that was your first-year decile, also pushing in the same concerning direction. Your family income level ranked third in influence, contributing toward the failure prediction, followed closely by your LSAT score doing the same. Your racial background came next, with a similar effect, and your undergraduate GPA rounded out the top factors, all tilting the scales against you. In contrast, your gender as female provided a small boost toward predicting success, as did your choice to go part-time, which helped counterbalance some risks compared to the full-time majority. Overall, your profile is stronger than average in key academic areas, yet the model weighed those high deciles and other strengths as potential vulnerabilities for bar passage in your specific combination. To boost your future chances, consider retaking the LSAT for an even higher score if possible, or strengthening any weak spots from undergrad if you can demonstrate improvement through additional coursework. Switching to full-time study might amplify the positive signal from your current setup, and focusing on bar-specific prep could address whatever the model sees as hidden risks in your grade patterns. With some targeted adjustments, you could shift that probability in your favor—keep pushing, as your foundation is already promising.
